text: This video demonstrates a technical standardized technique for a laparoscopic extended mesenterectomy in an ileocolic resection. There are two ways to perform an ileocolic resection. The first type is a close to bowel resection, which is the conventional technique wherein the vessels and nerves are preserved. The second type is an extended mesenterectomy in which the mesentery is taken conform an oncological operation. In order to preserve adequate vascularization on the colonic side, the mesentery is taken but the ileocolic trunk should be preserved. It is important that on the bowel side the operation is identical to the conventional technique and that not more bowel is removed.

The surgeon performs a single-port ileocolic resection with mesenterectomy, beginning by creating a submesenteric tunnel. The lateral peritoneal attachments are then dissected to achieve lateral mobilization. The proximal transaction line is marked, and the ileocolic trunk is identified to ensure it is spared during the extended mesenterectomy. A mesenteric window is created once the position of the ileocolic trunk is confirmed.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery from the ileocolic trunk, progressing towards the proximal transaction point. The dissection continues alongside the ileocolic trunk towards the colonic transaction point, revealing a triangular-shaped mesentery.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using clips after completing the extracorporeal fashioning of the anastomosis.
